items.prompt_md: In the grid-path counting table, `dp[r][c]` is built from the sum of which two cells?
options: dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1]
dp[r+1][c] and dp[r][c+1]
dp[r-1][c-1] only
dp[0][0] and dp[r][c]
items.answer: dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1]
items.explanation_md: Only two moves are legal — down or right — so the only ways to arrive at (r, c) are from the cell above or the cell to the left.

items.prompt_md: Why are the first row and first column of the grid-path table seeded with 1s instead of computed from the recurrence?
options: Because they have only one possible incoming direction, so there is exactly one way to reach any cell in them
Because 1 is an arbitrary placeholder that gets overwritten later
Because the recurrence divides by zero on the border
Because the problem always starts counting from 1, not 0
items.answer: Because they have only one possible incoming direction, so there is exactly one way to reach any cell in them
items.explanation_md: A cell on the first row can only have arrived from the left, and a cell on the first column only from above, so each has exactly one path — the base case the recurrence itself can't derive.

items.prompt_md: Which fill order guarantees dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1] are already computed when you need dp[r][c]?
options: Row-major order (top to bottom, left to right within each row)
Column-major order starting from the last column
Any order, since the table is symmetric
Reverse row-major order (bottom to top)
items.answer: Row-major order (top to bottom, left to right within each row)
items.explanation_md: Filling top-to-bottom, left-to-right ensures the cell above and the cell to the left are always computed strictly before the current cell.

items.prompt_md: How does the recurrence change at a cell that is a blocked obstacle in the grid?
options: dp[r][c] is forced to 0 regardless of dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1]
dp[r][c] is forced to 1 so the path count never drops to zero
The obstacle cell is skipped entirely and left uninitialized
The recurrence is unchanged; obstacles only affect the final answer
items.answer: dp[r][c] is forced to 0 regardless of dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1]
items.explanation_md: No path can end on a blocked cell, so its count of ways-to-reach is defined as 0, which then correctly propagates zero contribution to its neighbors.

items.prompt_md: Why can grid-path counting DP be rolled from O(m*n) space down to O(n) space?
options: Because each row only depends on the row directly above it and the running value to its left, so one row array can be overwritten in place
Because the answer only ever needs the last column, so earlier columns can be discarded immediately
Because the grid values themselves are never needed after the first pass
Because dp[r][c] does not actually depend on r once c is fixed
items.answer: Because each row only depends on the row directly above it and the running value to its left, so one row array can be overwritten in place
items.explanation_md: Since dp[r][c] only reads the previous row and the current row's own left neighbor, a single 1-D array updated left to right reproduces the same values without keeping the whole table.

items.prompt_md: In min-path-sum (minimize the sum of values along a path from top-left to bottom-right), how does the recurrence differ from unique-paths' counting recurrence?
options: It takes the min of the two incoming cells and adds the current cell's value, instead of summing the two incoming cells
It takes the max of the two incoming cells instead of the min
It multiplies the two incoming cells instead of adding them
It ignores the current cell's own value entirely
items.answer: It takes the min of the two incoming cells and adds the current cell's value, instead of summing the two incoming cells
items.explanation_md: Counting sums the number of ways in from each neighbor; minimizing a path cost instead picks whichever neighbor gives the cheaper path so far and adds this cell's own cost.

items.prompt_md: A common bug in grid-path DP is initializing the first row or column incorrectly when obstacles are involved. What is the correct behavior once an obstacle appears in the first row?
options: Every cell in that row from the obstacle onward must become 0, since a straight-line path can no longer reach them
Only the obstacle cell itself becomes 0; the rest of the row stays 1
The whole first row resets back to 1 after the obstacle
The obstacle in the first row has no effect on reachability
items.answer: Every cell in that row from the obstacle onward must become 0, since a straight-line path can no longer reach them
items.explanation_md: The first row/column only has one possible incoming direction; once that single path is blocked, every subsequent cell in the same line is permanently unreachable.

items.prompt_md: What does dp[r][c] represent, in words, for the unique-paths recurrence?
options: The number of distinct ways to reach cell (r, c) from the starting corner, moving only right or down
The shortest number of moves needed to reach cell (r, c)
Whether cell (r, c) lies on at least one path to the destination
The sum of all grid values on the way to cell (r, c)
items.answer: The number of distinct ways to reach cell (r, c) from the starting corner, moving only right or down
items.explanation_md: dp[r][c] is a count of ways-in, which is why it's built by summing the ways-in of its two legal predecessors.

items.prompt_md: A student fills the grid-path table column-major (down each column before moving to the next column) instead of row-major. Does this still produce a correct answer?
options: Yes, because column-major order still computes dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1] before dp[r][c] is needed
No, because dp[r][c-1] would not yet be computed when needed
No, because dp[r-1][c] would not yet be computed when needed
Only if the grid is square (m == n)
items.answer: Yes, because column-major order still computes dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1] before dp[r][c] is needed
items.explanation_md: Any fill order that visits both predecessors of a cell before the cell itself is safe; column-major (top to bottom within each column, moving left to right across columns) still respects that dependency, just like row-major does.

items.prompt_md: Why does grid-path counting DP avoid the exponential blowup of naive recursive path counting?
options: Each cell's answer is computed once and reused by every later cell that depends on it, instead of being re-derived from scratch each time
It skips cells that are far from the destination
It uses randomization to sample only a subset of paths
It sorts the grid values before processing
items.answer: Each cell's answer is computed once and reused by every later cell that depends on it, instead of being re-derived from scratch each time
items.explanation_md: Naive recursion re-explores the same sub-grid an exponential number of times; the DP table computes every cell exactly once and shares that result with every path that passes through it.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of filling the m x n dp table for unique paths?
options: O(m + n)
O(m * n)
O(m * n * log(m*n))
items.answer: O(m * n)
items.explanation_md: Every one of the m*n cells is computed exactly once from a constant number of neighbors, so the whole fill is O(m*n) time.

items.prompt_md: What is the space complexity of the full 2-D grid-path table before any rolling-array optimization?
options: O(m + n)
O(m * n)
items.answer: O(m * n)
items.explanation_md: The naive implementation allocates one entry per cell, so it uses O(m*n) space just like the time cost.

items.prompt_md: After rolling the grid-path table down to a single reused row array, what is the space complexity?
options: O(n)
O(m)
O(m * n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Only one row of length n needs to exist at a time, since each row only depends on the row directly above it.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the brute-force recursive path-counting solution without memoization?
options: O(m * n)
Exponential in m + n
O(log(m * n))
items.answer: Exponential in m + n
items.explanation_md: Without memoization the recursion re-explores overlapping sub-grids repeatedly, giving a branching factor of 2 at each of roughly m+n steps.

items.prompt_md: If obstacles are added to the grid but the recurrence and fill order stay the same, how does the time complexity of the DP solution change?
options: It stays O(m * n); each cell still does O(1) work, obstacle or not
It becomes O(m * n * k) where k is the number of obstacles
It becomes exponential because obstacles create branching
It drops to O(m + n) because obstacles prune most cells
items.answer: It stays O(m * n); each cell still does O(1) work, obstacle or not
items.explanation_md: Checking whether a cell is an obstacle and setting it to 0 is still O(1) per cell, so the asymptotic cost of the fill is unchanged.

items.prompt_md: A problem gives you an m x n grid and asks how many distinct ways there are to get from one corner to the other, moving only right or down. Which approach fits?
options: Recompute every path with plain, memo-less recursion
Build a 2-D table where each cell sums the ways in from its allowed neighbors
Sort the grid values and binary search for the target
Keep a hash map keyed by the path taken so far
items.answer: Build a 2-D table where each cell sums the ways in from its allowed neighbors
items.explanation_md: "How many ways through a grid with a fixed move set" is the fingerprint of grid-path counting DP: seed the first row/column, then fill every cell from its allowed neighbors.

items.prompt_md: You're given a grid of nonnegative costs and asked for the minimum total cost of a path from the top-left to the bottom-right cell, moving only right or down. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table where each cell takes the min of its two incoming neighbors plus its own cost
Greedy: always step into whichever adjacent cell has the smaller value
Backtracking that tries every path and keeps the cheapest
A single sweep with two pointers from opposite corners
items.answer: 2-D DP table where each cell takes the min of its two incoming neighbors plus its own cost
items.explanation_md: Two changing dimensions (row and column) with optimal-substructure costs is the grid-path DP trigger; a local greedy step can miss a cheaper path that requires a locally worse move.

items.prompt_md: You need to count the number of monotonic staircase paths in a grid, but this time the grid is effectively a single row (n = 1) and you're only choosing how to partition a 1-D sequence of steps. Which approach fits?
options: A 1-D DP array indexed by position along the single row
A 2-D DP table over two independent dimensions
Backtracking over all subsets of steps
A greedy scan taking the largest step available at each point
items.answer: A 1-D DP array indexed by position along the single row
items.explanation_md: With only one dimension actually varying, the state collapses to a single index — a 2-D table would carry a dummy dimension of size 1 for no benefit.

items.prompt_md: A grid-path problem now asks you to also print every distinct path (not just count them), and there could be exponentially many. Which approach fits for generating them?
options: Backtracking / try all — recursively build each path and collect it once you reach the destination
A 2-D DP counting table alone, since it already stores every path
Binary search over the grid dimensions
A single greedy pass that reconstructs one path
items.answer: Backtracking / try all — recursively build each path and collect it once you reach the destination
items.explanation_md: Enumerating every actual path (not just counting them) means you need all the exponentially many outputs, so backtracking is unavoidable here — a DP table only stores aggregate counts, not the paths themselves.

items.prompt_md: A problem gives you an m x n grid of positive integers and asks for the maximum sum you can collect on a path from top-left to bottom-right, moving only right or down. Which approach fits?
options: 2-D DP table where each cell takes the max of its two incoming neighbors plus its own value
Greedy: always step toward whichever neighbor has the larger value
A 1-D DP array, since only the final cell's value matters
Backtracking over every possible right/down path
items.answer: 2-D DP table where each cell takes the max of its two incoming neighbors plus its own value
items.explanation_md: Two changing dimensions (row, column) with an optimal-substructure recurrence is the grid-path DP fingerprint; a greedy largest-neighbor choice can lock in a locally big value that blocks a globally better path.

items.prompt_md: You're given an m x n grid and asked for the number of distinct paths from top-left to bottom-right, moving only right or down, but you must compute the answer modulo a large prime because the raw count overflows. Which approach fits?
options: The same 2-D DP table, just taking each cell's sum modulo the prime as you go
Switch to backtracking so you can count paths one at a time without overflow
A 1-D DP array, since modulo arithmetic removes the need for two dimensions
Binary search on the answer to find the count modulo the prime
items.answer: The same 2-D DP table, just taking each cell's sum modulo the prime as you go
items.explanation_md: Needing the answer modulo a prime doesn't change the dependency structure at all — it's still two changing dimensions with a fixed neighbor set, just with an extra modulo operation applied at each cell.

items.prompt_md: You're asked how many ways there are to tile a 2 x n board with 1x2 dominoes. Each column's placement only depends on how the previous one or two columns were filled. Which approach fits?
options: A 1-D DP array indexed by column, since the state is fully captured by a small window of previous columns
A full 2-D DP table over two grid coordinates like grid-path counting
Backtracking over every tiling arrangement
A greedy left-to-right placement of dominoes
items.answer: A 1-D DP array indexed by column, since the state is fully captured by a small window of previous columns
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: even though a grid is involved, the state collapses to a single position along one axis (the column index), so a 1-D array — not a full 2-D table — is enough.

items.prompt_md: You must count the number of distinct paths through a grid, moving only right or down, where certain cells are blocked and can never be stepped on. Which approach fits?
options: The same 2-D DP table, but forcing dp[r][c] = 0 at every blocked cell
Skip the DP table entirely and just subtract the blocked cells from the unobstructed answer
Switch to a 1-D DP array since obstacles reduce the effective grid size
Backtracking that tries every path and discards ones hitting an obstacle
items.answer: The same 2-D DP table, but forcing dp[r][c] = 0 at every blocked cell
items.explanation_md: Obstacles don't change the underlying two-dimension dependency structure — they just override individual cells to 0 so paths through them contribute nothing.

items.prompt_md: A robot on an m x n grid can move right or down, and every cell has a fixed reward; you want the maximum total reward but you are only allowed to visit at most one cell per row (any column). Which approach fits?
options: A 1-D DP array over rows, since only the best value from the previous row matters, not a specific column dependency chain
A 2-D DP table where dp[r][c] depends on dp[r-1][c] and dp[r][c-1]
Backtracking that enumerates every column choice per row
Sorting each row and greedily picking the largest reward
items.answer: A 1-D DP array over rows, since only the best value from the previous row matters, not a specific column dependency chain
items.explanation_md: This is a trap: because any column can be chosen independently in each row (there's no left/right adjacency constraint), the state reduces to "best value seen in the previous row," which a 1-D array captures without needing per-column dependencies.

items.prompt_md: You're given an m x n grid and asked to count paths from top-left to bottom-right where movement is right, down, or diagonal (down-right). Which approach fits?
options: A 2-D DP table where dp[r][c] sums dp[r-1][c], dp[r][c-1], and dp[r-1][c-1]
A 1-D DP array over the anti-diagonals only
Backtracking that explores every combination of the three move types
BFS layer by layer from the start cell
items.answer: A 2-D DP table where dp[r][c] sums dp[r-1][c], dp[r][c-1], and dp[r-1][c-1]
items.explanation_md: Adding a diagonal move just adds a third predecessor term to the same 2-D table discipline — the two-dimension, fixed-neighbor-set structure that defines grid-path DP is unchanged.
